citation: Neuhaus EM, Zhang W, Gelis L, Deng Y, Noldus J, Hatt H (2009). "Activation of an olfactory receptor inhibits proliferation of prostate cancer cells." J Biol Chem 284(24):16218-16225.
claim: Activation of OR51E2 (PSGR) by specific steroid ligands (ADT/1,4,6-androstatriene-3,17-dione, 6-dehydrotestosterone, 1,4,6-androstatriene-17beta-ol-3-one) or the odorant beta-ionone inhibits proliferation of prostate cancer cells (LNCaP, primary prostate epithelial cells) by up to ~50% (144h, 250uM beta-ionone). PC-3 cells (which do not express PSGR) and HEK293 cells (PSGR-negative) show no proliferation effect, confirming receptor specificity.
mechanism: Beta-ionone (active from ~100nM) and ADT (active in the nanomolar range) trigger intracellular Ca2+ increase via PSGR; alpha-ionone blocks beta-ionone's Ca2+ response and does not activate PSGR alone. CORRECTION from original abstract-derived summary: LNCaP cells showed NO modulation of ERK1/2 (p44/42) after ligand exposure (explicitly tested and negative) -- the actual MAPK pathway activated is p38 and SAPK/JNK specifically (phosphorylated within 5-10 min, elevated up to 30 min). p38 inhibitor reverses the antiproliferative effect near its IC50 (used 0.5uM, IC50=0.38uM); SAPK/JNK inhibitor requires relatively high concentration (used 1uM vs IC50=40nM for JNK-1/2, 90nM for JNK3) to be effective. PSGR-siRNA knockdown in LNCaP cells reduces beta-ionone-induced Ca2+ signal by ~80%, confirming the response is PSGR-dependent, not an off-target chemical effect.
model_system: LNCaP cells (endogenous receptor); also HEK293 and PC-3 cells, and primary prostate epithelial cells
verification_notes: Cross-checked against CLAUDE.md Section 6 table and the friend's (Bansal/Gudisay) Literature_Review.docx summary, which independently confirms cell lines, ligands, and MAPK/Ca2+ mechanism in more methodological detail than the bare citation. Single primary study on this specific claim (no independent replication located yet) -- treat as unreplicated pending further search. FULL-TEXT VERIFICATION (2026-07-12, live pull of the PMC web-rendered article at pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2713531/ -- note this succeeded even though the PMC efetch XML API for this same ID returns metadata-only with no <body>; the two access paths are NOT equivalent for this record). This full-text read corrects and substantially enriches the original abstract/secondary-summary-derived record: (1) MECHANISM CORRECTION -- p44/42 (ERK1/2) was explicitly tested and showed NO modulation in LNCaP; only p38 and SAPK/JNK were activated. The original mechanism text listing p44/42 alongside p38/SAPK-JNK as if all three were activated was inaccurate (it derived from an antibody list used for validation, not from the actual positive-result pathway). (2) NEW N VALUES: siRNA-knockdown Ca2+ quantification used n=22 siRNA-expressing vs n=186 control cells (beta-ionone) and n=26 vs n=201 (ADT), each from four independent transfections/experiments -- filled into sample_size/replication_count above (100+ cells analyzed per condition, 3 repeat experiments minimum referenced across figures). (3) Specific ligand names identified (not just 'androstenone derivatives'): ADT, 6-dehydrotestosterone, 1,4,6-androstatriene-17-ol-3-one. (4) Two additional sub-claims recorded separately below: the siRNA receptor-specificity knockdown control, and the MAPK-inhibitor pharmacological rescue with IC50 data.
raw_excerpt_or_link: doi:10.1074/jbc.M109.012096; corroborating summary in data/intake/or51e2/Literature Review.docx

citation: Sanz G, Leray I, Dewaele A, Sobilo J, Lerondel S, Bouet S, Grebert D, Monnerie R, Pajot-Augy E, Mir LM (2014). "Promotion of cancer cell invasiveness and metastasis emergence caused by olfactory receptor stimulation." PLoS ONE 9(1):e85110.
claim: Beta-ionone stimulation of PSGR/OR51E2 promotes LNCaP cell invasiveness; co-administration of alpha-ionone (a PSGR antagonist) abrogates this effect.
mechanism: Beta-ionone (100 uM) increased LNCaP invasion index (collagen type I gel invasion assay) by a factor of ~2.75, via a PI3K-gamma / Gbeta-gamma G-protein pathway (blocked by the PI3Kgamma inhibitor AS605240 and by the Gbeta-gamma inhibitor gallein). Alpha-ionone alone had no effect but fully blocked beta-ionone's pro-invasive effect when co-administered.
model_system: LNCaP cells
verification_notes: RESOLVED (priority-one verification task from CLAUDE.md Section 6/12): pulled and read the actual Sanz et al. 2014 full text via PLoS ONE (not the friend's intake materials -- a similarly titled melanoma paper in Literature_Review.docx is a different study, Gelis et al. 2017, see below; do not conflate). Sanz measures INVASIVENESS (collagen-gel invasion index), not proliferation -- a different endpoint from Neuhaus 2009's proliferation assay. The Sanz authors explicitly acknowledge the apparent conflict in their own discussion: beta-ionone's pro-invasive effect here is 'not in agreement with the results of Neuhaus et al.' on proliferation, and they frame this as two distinct, potentially co-occurring phenotypes (reduced proliferation + increased invasiveness is a recognized cancer biology pattern), not necessarily a same-endpoint contradiction. CONCLUSION: this is a real, citable divergence in the literature on whether activating OR51E2 is net beneficial or harmful therapeutically, but it is nuanced rather than a flat contradiction -- both effects (anti-proliferative AND pro-invasive) could be simultaneously true. Present it to Person B/the demo as 'evidence is contested on clinical implication, though the mechanism papers do not strictly contradict each other on a shared endpoint' rather than a clean two-sided contradiction. UPDATE (found via K Pro baseline capture, cross-checked): Sanz et al.'s own 2016 Oncotarget follow-up revises the alpha-ionone characterization used in this 2014 paper -- alpha-ionone is NOT simply a PSGR antagonist as stated here; the 2016 paper found it is a full agonist that promotes tumor GROWTH via a separate pathway from beta-ionone's pro-invasive effect. The claim/mechanism text below reflects the 2014 paper's own characterization at time of publication; see the linked 2016 record for the corrected picture. Do not present alpha-ionone as an antagonist without this caveat. FULL-TEXT FIGURE VERIFICATION (PMC3885679, live pull): the in vitro LNCaP invasion assay (Figure 2c) explicitly reports n=3 with a two-tailed Student's t-test, control SD=13.42%. This paper studies TWO cell systems in parallel (BON neuroendocrine cells with OR1G1/OR17-40, and LNCaP with PSGR/OR51E2) -- do not confuse the BON-cell invasion factors (2.7x/2.5x) with the LNCaP/PSGR invasion factor (2.75x) reported for this record; they are from different receptor/cell-line pairs in the same paper. Three additional sub-claims from this paper are recorded as separate evidence records below (in vivo mouse metastasis, PI3K-gamma/Gbeta-gamma mechanism rescue, and a mineral-oil vehicle confound / negative-result finding) rather than folded into this one, since they measure genuinely distinct endpoints.

citation: Rodriguez M, Luo W, Weng J, Zeng L, Yi Z, Siwko S, Liu M (2014). "PSGR promotes prostatic intraepithelial neoplasia and prostate cancer xenograft growth through NF-kappaB." Oncogenesis 3(8):e114.
claim: PSGR (OR51E2) overexpression drives premalignant prostate lesions and larger xenograft tumors.
mechanism: Probasin-promoter-driven PSGR-transgenic mice developed chronic inflammatory response leading to prostatic intraepithelial neoplasia (PIN) with age. PSGR-overexpressing LNCaP xenografts formed larger tumors than control LNCaP xenografts. NF-kappaB/RELA identified as the key downstream signaling node.
model_system: PSGR-transgenic mouse model (probasin promoter); LNCaP xenograft (mouse)
verification_notes: Confirmed via PubMed/Oncogenesis record (PMID 25111863; PMC5189964) -- study design, NF-kB mechanism, and xenograft growth-promotion finding all match CLAUDE.md Section 6 summary. FULL-TEXT FIGURE VERIFICATION (PMC5189964, live pull): xenograft tumor-size comparison (Figure 5, orthotopic LNCaP-Ctrl vs. LNCaP-PSGR implantation) used n=7 mice per group (urinary system weight, P=0.023; morphologic tumor-formation difference, P=0.001). The transgenic-mouse inflammatory cytokine/NF-kB analysis (Figure 3a) used three mice per genotype per experiment (P=0.01 and P=0.001 for specific cytokines). Figure 4a additionally shows PTEN (phosphatase and tensin homolog) expression is altered alongside PSGR in prostate cancer cell lines -- a specific tumor-suppressor-pathway interaction not captured in the original abstract-derived claim above, worth a follow-up record if PTEN status becomes relevant to interpreting this receptor's role.

citation: Pronin A, Slepak V (2021). "Ectopically expressed olfactory receptors OR51E1 and OR51E2 suppress proliferation and promote cell death in a prostate cancer cell line." J Biol Chem 296:100475.
claim: Inducible OR51E1/OR51E2 expression in LNCaP cells suppresses proliferation and promotes cell death; the paper explicitly flags field-wide controversy over whether beta-ionone is a genuine OR51E2 agonist.
mechanism: Inducible expression system in LNCaP cells; OR51E1 responds to short/medium-chain organic acids (C3-C9, not acetate), OR51E2 responds to acetate and propionate but not longer-chain acids -- a distinct ligand profile from the androstenone/beta-ionone activation reported by Neuhaus. Paper states beta-ionone agonism of OR51E2 has not been replicated by all groups and attributes the field's controversy to technical difficulties in detecting/functionally assaying endogenous vs. overexpressed ORs.
model_system: LNCaP cells (inducible expression system)
verification_notes: Confirmed via PubMed/PMC (PMC8024707) and J Biol Chem fulltext -- ligand-specificity finding and explicit controversy statement both match CLAUDE.md Section 6 summary. This paper's own ligand-specificity data (OR51E2 = acetate/propionate, not beta-ionone) is itself a relevant data point for interpreting whether Neuhaus/Sanz's beta-ionone results generalize.

citation: Gelis L, Jovancevic N, Veitinger S, Mandal B, Arndt HD, Neuhaus EM, Hatt H (2017). "Functional expression of olfactory receptors in human primary melanoma and melanoma metastasis." Exp Dermatol 26(7):569-576.
claim: OR51E2 is upregulated in melanoma tissue and metastasis vs. normal melanocytes; activation by beta-ionone inhibits proliferation of vertical-growth-phase (VGP) melanoma cells via apoptosis and reduces cell motility.
mechanism: Beta-ionone activation of endogenous OR51E2 in cultured melanoma cells triggers an intracellular Ca2+ increase (confirmed OR51E2-dependent via RNAi knockdown), which drives apoptosis in VGP melanoma cells and reduces migration.
model_system: Human primary melanoma tissue and melanoma metastasis samples; cultured VGP melanoma cells
verification_notes: Sourced from the friend's (Bansal/Gudisay) Literature_Review.docx summary, cross-checked against the title/journal (Exp Dermatol 26:569-576) to confirm this is Gelis et al. 2017, NOT Sanz et al. 2014 -- the two have superficially similar-sounding titles ('functional expression...melanoma' vs. 'promotion of cancer cell invasiveness...') but are distinct studies with distinct findings; do not conflate. Direction (tumor-suppressive) is consistent with Neuhaus 2009 and Pronin & Slepak 2021, adding a second cancer-type data point in the same direction.

citation: Jovancevic N, Dendorfer A, Matzkies M, et al. (2017). "Medium-chain fatty acid derived receptor OR51E2 agonist beta-ionone regulates RPE cell migration and proliferation." Frontiers in Physiology.
claim: OR51E2 is expressed in human retinal pigment epithelial (RPE) and choroid cells; beta-ionone activation INCREASES RPE cell proliferation and migration (opposite direction from the cancer-tissue findings above).
mechanism: Beta-ionone triggers Ca2+ influx (primarily extracellular, secondarily from intracellular stores), elevation of cAMP via adenylyl cyclase activation, and phosphorylation of ERK1/2 and AKT, which together enhance RPE cell proliferation and migration in a dose-dependent manner.
model_system: RPE cells (non-cancer)
verification_notes: Sourced from the friend's Literature_Review.docx summary. Non-cancer tissue context. Direction is neutral (not a tumor claim) and direction_context=supporting_evidence so this does not enter the activation mass or contradiction detection. Mechanistically relevant context: beta-ionone activates OR51E2 in RPE cells to increase proliferation/migration, opposite to the Neuhaus/Gelis cancer findings — useful background for understanding ligand-dependent direction context, not a cancer-outcome claim.

citation: Bansal S, Gudisay S (2025). Dual-luciferase reporter assay data, unpublished (ISEF 2025 CBIO034T / ACSEF HS-BCOM-296-T2 / IFoRE 2025 project: "Allosteric Regulation of Ectopically Expressed Olfactory Receptors in Tumor Cells").
claim: Screened four candidate ligands (methylmalonic acid, 2,2-difluorocyclopropane-1-carboxylate, 3,3-difluoropropanoic acid, 4,4-difluoropentanoic acid) against a propionic acid positive control for OR51E2 activation, using a CREB-driven firefly luciferase / constitutive NanoLuc dual-reporter assay in transiently transfected HEK293 cells (pCI-OR51E2 + pGL4.53[luc2/PGK] + pCRE-Luc), at 0.5M and 1M ligand concentrations.
mechanism: CREB-driven firefly luciferase reports downstream GPCR/CREB pathway activation; constitutive NanoLuc serves as a transfection/expression control. Relative response computed as (firefly/NanoLuc) normalized to the positive control's ratio.
model_system: HEK293 cells, transient transfection (heterologous OR51E2 expression)
verification_notes: IMPORTANT DATA-QUALITY FINDING: the ISEF video script claims '2 of 4 ligands successfully activated the receptor,' and the spreadsheets (firefly.xlsx, nano-luc.xlsx) contain a partially pre-computed 'RRR' (relative response ratio) column. I recomputed the ratios independently from the raw RLU columns rather than trusting the stated conclusion (per CLAUDE.md's personal-verification requirement). Findings: (1) the RRR values present in the sheet correspond to a NanoLuc/Firefly ratio normalized to positive control -- e.g. at 1M, methylmalonic acid=1.02, 2,2-difluorocyclopropane-1-carboxylate=0.81, 3,3-difluoropropanoic acid=1.31, 4,4-difluoropentanoic acid=0.98 -- none of which stands out as a clear >2x activation vs. control the way the video script implies. (2) At 0.5M, using the same ratio direction, computed relative values are far larger (roughly 5.8x, 19.5x, 99x, and 367x for the four ligands respectively) -- an inconsistency in scale between the two concentrations that is NOT explained by the data as entered, and strongly suggests either a well-position/labeling error in the raw plate export or that the two concentration blocks are not directly comparable as recorded. (3) The 'RLU'/'Drug Name' summary block at the top of each sheet contains values that are internally inconsistent with the 'Well'-labeled block below it in the same file (e.g. duplicate numbers appearing under different well/concentration labels), which further undermines confidence in a clean readout. CONCLUSION: do not import '2 of 4 ligands activated' as a stated finding. This is genuinely inconclusive from the data as provided -- direction set to 'unclear' and recommend requesting the original plate-reader export files (not the transcribed spreadsheet) before this record can support a real activation/no-activation claim. Treat as N=1, unreplicated, contested-by-recomputation.

citation: US10588884B2, "Modulators of prostate-specific G-protein receptor (PSGR/OR51E2) and methods of using same" -- Duke University (Abaffy T, Matsunami H). Filed 2017-11-01, granted 2020-03-17.
claim: Discloses 28 OR51E2 modulators (agonists and 1 antagonist, 13-cis retinoic acid/isotretinoin) including 19-OH AD, AFMK, glycyl-glycine, kojibiose, estriol, pelargonidin, palmitic acid, claiming methods to treat/prevent castrate-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC) and diagnose prostate cancer via metabolite biomarkers. The 28 patent compounds are distinct from ChEMBL's 26 unique molecules — do not conflate these two datasets.
mechanism: Chronic agonist-mediated OR51E2 activation facilitates cellular transformation toward an aggressive neuroendocrine-like phenotype resistant to androgen deprivation therapy; antagonists are claimed to block this transformation.
model_system: Patent disclosure (in vitro/in vivo data referenced in specification, not independently verified here)
verification_notes: Confirmed via Google Patents full text and cross-referenced against the live ChEMBL pull for OR51E2 (CHEMBL4523454) -- several compounds named in this patent (glycine, estriol, pelargonidin, palmitic acid, isotretinoin) are the exact same compounds appearing as EC50/IC50-annotated ChEMBL activity records, confirming the ChEMBL bioactivity data traces back to this patent's disclosed screen. Per CLAUDE.md Section 3: patents are evidence of commercial interest, not independent biological validation -- this claim (chronic activation -> tumor-promoting/NEtD) should not be folded into the literature-consensus weighting alongside Neuhaus/Sanz/Rodriguez/Pronin.

citation: US12186294B2, "Modulators of prostate-specific g-protein receptor (PSGR/OR51E2) and methods of using same" -- Duke University (Abaffy T, Matsunami H). Divisional of US10588884B2 (Ser. No. 15/801,258, priority to provisional 62/415,591 filed 2016-11-01). Filed 2020-02-03, granted 2025-01-07, expires 2038-10-27.
claim: Divisional continuation of US10588884B2 covering the same 28 compounds (agonists/1 antagonist), expanding claims to metabolomic-profiling diagnostic methods and describing a proposed link between Propionibacterium acnes infection and prostate cancer progression via OR51E2 activation.
mechanism: Same as US10588884B2, plus a proposed infection-driven activation route (P. acnes metabolites as endogenous OR51E2 agonists).
model_system: Patent disclosure (in vitro/in vivo data referenced in specification, not independently verified here)
verification_notes: Confirmed via Google Patents full text. Same commercial-interest caveat as the parent patent -- do not fold into literature-consensus weighting. The P. acnes connection is a novel, low-confidence claim worth flagging as exploratory context, not an established mechanism.

citation: GDC API cnvs endpoint, gene ENSG00000167332, live pull
claim: OR51E2 CNV gain/amplification observed across 45 TCGA projects, 1092 cases total. Top projects by case count: TCGA-BRCA (n=124), TCGA-LUAD (n=80), CPTAC-3 (n=70), TCGA-OV (n=65), TCGA-STAD (n=59).
model_system: TCGA pan-cancer cohort (CNV calls)
verification_notes: Live GDC API pull (api.gdc.cancer.gov/cnvs), gene_id=ENSG00000167332, cnv_change=Gain. Counts are de-duplicated by unique case_id (not raw CNV segment records, which undercounts -- the API stores one segment record per distinct call shared across many cases via an occurrence array). Direction is 'unclear' at the CNV level alone -- amplification signal does not by itself establish tumor-promoting/suppressive direction, that requires the functional literature records in this file.

citation: GDC API ssms endpoint, gene ENSG00000167332, live pull
claim: OR51E2 somatic mutations observed in 217 cases across 31 TCGA projects.
model_system: TCGA pan-cancer cohort (simple somatic mutation calls)
verification_notes: Live GDC API pull (api.gdc.cancer.gov/ssms). Low mutation burden across olfactory receptors in cancer is generally expected/consistent with a role driven by ectopic expression/overexpression rather than coding mutation -- worth flagging if a receptor bucks this pattern.

citation: PDB 8F76 -- Billesbolle, C.B. et al. (2023). "Structural basis of odorant recognition by a human odorant receptor." Nature 615:742-749.
claim: Solved structure of Human olfactory receptor OR51E2 bound to propionate in complex with miniGs399, method=ELECTRON MICROSCOPY, resolution=3.1 A, released 2023-03-22. This is a structural cross-check only -- it does not itself make a claim about tumor direction/mechanism.
model_system: ELECTRON MICROSCOPY structure (Human olfactory receptor OR51E2 bound to propionate in complex with miniGs399)
verification_notes: Live RCSB PDB REST API pull (full-text search for 'OR51E2', matched entry 8F76). doi:10.1038/s41586-023-05798-y.

citation: ChEMBL target CHEMBL4523454 (Olfactory receptor 51E2), live API pull
claim: ChEMBL database contains 26 unique molecules tested against OR51E2, with 25 EC50/IC50 potency measurements across 144 individual assay data records. IMPORTANT: the 144 is the number of raw assay-database entries (multiple records per compound), NOT the number of distinct compounds — there are 26 unique molecules. This is entirely separate from the Duke/Abaffy patent (US10588884B2) which discloses 28 compounds. Do not conflate these two sources.
model_system: ChEMBL-aggregated heterologous expression assays (multiple cell systems, see individual assay_description fields)
verification_notes: Live ChEMBL REST API pull (target_chembl_id=CHEMBL4523454). This is a ligand-binding/potency data source, not a tumor-direction claim -- grounds rule-generation claims about ligand potency in real binding data rather than inferred from paper discussion sections, per CLAUDE.md Section 8's rationale for adding ChEMBL.

citation: Kim JS et al. (2025). "beta-Ionone suppresses colorectal tumorigenesis by activating OR51E2, a potential tumor suppressor." Phytomedicine. doi:10.1016/j.phymed.2025.156599
claim: beta-Ionone activates OR51E2 in colorectal cancer cells, elevating intracellular Ca2+, inhibiting MEK/ERK phosphorylation, suppressing proliferation, inducing apoptosis, and reducing xenograft tumor growth in an OR51E2-dependent manner.
mechanism: Ca2+ elevation → MEK/ERK inhibition; OR51E2 mRNA downregulated in CRC via m6A modification (METTL3/14-YTHDF axis); STM2457 (METTL3 inhibitor) restores OR51E2 expression and suppresses proliferation
model_system: CRC cell lines (OR51E2 knockdown); xenograft nude mice (5 and 10 mg/kg beta-ionone)
verification_notes: AUTO-EXTRACTED from PubMed abstract (PMID 40088737). Full text not yet read. Key claims (Ca2+/MEK-ERK mechanism, xenograft reduction, m6A regulatory mechanism) match abstract text. Direction and endpoint are clear from abstract. Queue for Person A full-text verification before use in high-confidence claims.

citation: Kim JS et al. (2025). "Propionate suppresses colitis-associated colorectal cancer in mice by Or51e2." Biomedicine & pharmacotherapy. doi:10.1016/j.biopha.2025.118268
claim: Propionate (SCFA) activates Or51e2 in colorectal cancer, increasing cAMP, inhibiting cell proliferation, inducing apoptosis, and suppressing MEK/ERK signaling; in vivo, propionate reduced tumor burden in AOM/DSS colitis-associated CRC model in WT but not Or51e2-knockout mice.
mechanism: Propionate → Or51e2 → cAMP elevation → MEK/ERK suppression; loss of effect in Or51e2 KO mice confirms receptor dependence; propionate is a SCFA from dietary fiber fermentation
model_system: CT26 murine CRC cells; AOM/DSS colitis-associated CRC model (WT and Or51e2 KO mice)
verification_notes: AUTO-EXTRACTED from PubMed abstract (PMID 40532574). Full text not yet read. The KO mouse validation is particularly strong evidence — loss of propionate effect in Or51e2 KO confirms receptor-specific mechanism, not off-target. Direction and endpoint are clear. Queue for Person A full-text verification before use in high-confidence claims.

citation: Sanz G, Le Poupon C, Bouet S, et al. (2016). "Alpha-ionone is a full agonist of the prostate-specific G-protein coupled receptor (PSGR/OR51E2) that promotes tumor growth." Oncotarget.
claim: Alpha-ionone, previously characterized (Sanz 2014) as blocking beta-ionone's pro-invasive effect, is actually a full OR51E2 agonist in its own right that promotes tumor GROWTH (not invasion) via a distinct pathway from beta-ionone. Combined alpha+beta-ionone treatment increases total tumor burden more than either ligand alone.
mechanism: Distinct downstream coupling from beta-ionone's PI3K-gamma/invasion axis -- growth-promoting signaling, not invasion-promoting. Supports biased agonism at OR51E2 (same receptor, different ligands recruiting different effector pathways with different phenotypic outcomes).
model_system: LNCaP cells / xenograft
verification_notes: NOT YET independently verified against full text -- added from K Pro's baseline-capture answer during live testing, which cited this as a follow-up to Sanz 2014 (114 citations on the 2014 paper; this 2016 follow-up reported ~30 citations per K Pro's summary). Corrects the 'alpha-ionone = antagonist' claim in the Sanz 2014 record. Treat as preliminary/single-source until someone reads the actual Oncotarget paper -- this is flagged honestly as unverified, consistent with the project's confidence-qualification discipline, not silently upgraded to verified status.

citation: Thomsen ARB, et al. (2025). "CRISPR-Cas9 knockout of OR51E2 in prostate cancer cells reveals a tumor-suppressive role." BMC Cancer.
claim: CRISPR-Cas9 knockout of OR51E2 in LNCaP cells INCREASES proliferation, migration, adhesion, anchorage-independent colony formation, and tumor growth -- i.e. loss of OR51E2 produces a more aggressive phenotype. In the TCGA PRAD cohort, low OR51E2 expression correlates with worse prognosis and higher Gleason grade.
mechanism: STAT3/IL-6 inflammatory pathway implicated by omics analysis following knockout.
model_system: LNCaP cells (CRISPR-Cas9 knockout); cross-referenced against TCGA-PRAD cohort
verification_notes:
NOT YET independently verified against full text -- added from K Pro baseline capture. This is a literature claim that directly references the same TCGA-PRAD cohort our TCGA pull already covers (see scripts/tcga_pull.py output) -- worth cross-checking this claim's TCGA correlation against our own live TCGA-PRAD pull if time allows, since that would be a genuine literature-to-data cross-validation, not just another citation. Flagged as unverified/single-source until full text is read.

TCGA CROSS-VALIDATION (computed, 500 TCGA-PRAD cases): conclusion=CONTRADICTS_THOMSEN. CNV-Gain cases (proxy for higher expression) have HIGHER mean Gleason group (4.35) than CNV-Neutral cases (3.14), which is the OPPOSITE of Thomsen's claim. Note: CNV gain ≠ RNA expression — this is a proxy check only, not a direct expression measurement. Treat as a signal, not a refutation. CNV-Loss cases (proxy for lower expression): mean Gleason 4.06 (n=17). This is a CNV-based proxy check, not a direct expression-vs-Gleason test. The Thomsen claim is about RNA expression level, which requires downloading STAR-counts files from GDC (not accessible via simple REST API). CNV gain correlates with expression on average but is not equivalent. Treat this as a corroborating signal or a red flag, not a definitive confirmation or refutation.
endpoint: proliferation, migration, tumor growth, prognosis correlation

citation: Marelli G, Morina N, Portale F, et al. (2025). "Palmitic acid activates OR51E2 on tumor-associated macrophages to promote immunosuppression and cancer progression." Nature Immunology.
claim: In the tumor microenvironment (TME), palmitic acid binds OR51E2 on tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) — NOT tumor cells — and enhances their pro-tumor phenotype. In vivo deletion of OR51E2 (and other chemosensors) in TAMs caused cancer regression and increased CD8+ T cell infiltration.
mechanism: Non-cell-autonomous: palmitic acid (a fatty acid metabolite abundant in the TME) activates OR51E2 on macrophages, polarizing TAMs toward an immunosuppressive M2-like state. Effect is distinct from the cell-autonomous OR51E2 signaling in prostate epithelial cells studied by Neuhaus/Sanz/Rodriguez.
model_system: Human prostate tissue / mouse TAM-specific OR51E2 deletion model
verification_notes: NOT independently verified against full text -- added from K Pro baseline-capture answer during live testing (6 citations per K Pro summary, very recent 2025). Treat as preliminary/single-source pending full-text read. ONTOLOGY NOTE: This claim is structurally different from all other OR51E2 records in this file. The current graph schema (discordance/ontology.py) has no node type for CellType (immune vs epithelial), no edge property for "non-cell-autonomous", and no way to distinguish OR51E2 in TAMs vs OR51E2 in tumor cells. Forcing this into the current schema would silently misrepresent it as the same kind of claim as Neuhaus/Sanz -- the current ModelSystem node would be "human prostate tissue / mouse TAM" which partially captures it but does not distinguish the immune-cell-specific mechanism. A real ontology extension would need: (1) a CellType node (e.g. TumorCell vs Macrophage/ImmuneCell), (2) a new direction_context = "non_cell_autonomous" or a new edge type "receptor_cell_type", (3) possibly a new Direction value like "immunosuppressive" distinct from "tumor_promoting". Estimated work: ~2-3 hours to extend ontology.py, models.py, contradiction.py, and the graph builder correctly. RECOMMENDATION: Load this record as direction=tumor-promoting with a strong confidence_note about the ontology gap rather than silently forcing a bad fit or skipping the record.
raw_excerpt_or_link: Cited by K Pro baseline (2025 Nature Immunology, PMID not yet confirmed)

citation: Sanz G, Leray I, Dewaele A, et al. (2014). "Promotion of cancer cell invasiveness and metastasis emergence caused by olfactory receptor stimulation." PLoS ONE 9(1):e85110 -- Figures 4-5 (in vivo xenograft/metastasis model).
claim: LNCaP prostate tumor cells inoculated subcutaneously into immunodeficient NSG mice and treated daily (skin application) with PSGR agonist beta-ionone in mineral oil show altered metastasis pattern vs. untreated and vehicle-only controls: metastases to lungs and Tyson glands occurred ONLY in beta-ionone-treated mice (3/5 animals for Tyson glands, 2/5 for lungs), and cumulative metastasis count over time was higher in beta-ionone-treated mice than controls. Notably, tumor take rate was LOWER in treated groups (50% mineral oil/beta-ionone vs. 85% untreated), suggesting beta-ionone/PSGR stimulation promotes metastatic spread from established tumors rather than initial tumor formation.
mechanism: PSGR (OR51E2) expression confirmed in primary tumors and all metastases by immunohistochemistry (anti-PSGR, anti-PSA). No molecular mechanism assayed in vivo beyond confirming receptor expression -- mechanism inference relies on the in vitro PI3K-gamma/Gbeta-gamma findings from the same paper (see separate record).
model_system: LNCaP xenograft, NSG (NOD scid gamma) immunodeficient mice, subcutaneous inoculation, daily topical beta-ionone in mineral oil
verification_notes: NEW record from full-text figure/results read (PMC3885679, live pull) -- this in vivo metastasis-model data was NOT captured in the original abstract-derived Sanz record above; abstracts do not report in vivo N or the tumor-take-rate finding. n=5 mice per group confirmed from Results text ('3 out of 5 animals for Tyson glands and 2 out of 5 animals for lungs'). This is a substantially stronger tumor-promoting claim than the in vitro invasion assay alone (Figure 2c) since it demonstrates actual increased metastatic spread in a live-animal model, not just an in vitro invasion-index proxy. IMPORTANT CONFOUND (see separate mineral-oil record): the vehicle (mineral oil) alone also increased metastasis counts vs. untreated controls, so part of this effect is not attributable to beta-ionone specifically -- the paper's own framing is that beta-ionone adds an effect ON TOP OF a mineral-oil baseline effect (more metastases, and uniquely to lungs/Tyson glands), not that mineral oil is a clean negative control.
raw_excerpt_or_link: doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0085110, Figures 4-5 and accompanying Results text

citation: Sanz G, Leray I, Dewaele A, et al. (2014). "Promotion of cancer cell invasiveness and metastasis emergence caused by olfactory receptor stimulation." PLoS ONE 9(1):e85110 -- Figure 3 (PI3K-gamma / Gbeta-gamma mechanism in LNCaP).
claim: PI3K-gamma inhibitor AS605240 and Gbeta-gamma inhibitor gallein both counteract beta-ionone-induced LNCaP invasiveness, confirming PI3K-gamma/Gbeta-gamma pathway involvement downstream of PSGR activation. Notably, AS605240 also reduced LNCaP invasiveness even WITHOUT beta-ionone stimulation, implying PI3K-gamma contributes to LNCaP's basal invasiveness independent of PSGR.
mechanism: PSGR (OR51E2) activation by beta-ionone signals through Gbeta-gamma G-protein subunits to PI3K-gamma, promoting invasiveness; gallein (Gbeta-gamma inhibitor) and AS605240 (PI3K-gamma inhibitor) both block this. In the paper's parallel BON-cell/OR1G1 system, the same inhibitors produced an ~80% (not total) reduction in odorant-induced invasiveness at the same doses.
model_system: LNCaP cells, collagen type I gel invasion assay
verification_notes: NEW record from full-text figure read (PMC3885679, live pull), n=3 confirmed in caption ('bars indicate standard deviation (n = 3)', two-tailed Student's t-test; control SD=4.74% for BON cells, 13.86% for LNCaP cells). The paper reports an explicit ~80% reduction figure for the BON-cell/OR1G1 system but does NOT give an equivalent percentage for the LNCaP/PSGR system -- text says the PI3K-gamma/gallein result 'was confirmed for LNCaP cells' without a specific number, so don't overstate this as quantitatively matched to the BON-cell figure.

citation: Sanz G, Leray I, Dewaele A, et al. (2014). "Promotion of cancer cell invasiveness and metastasis emergence caused by olfactory receptor stimulation." PLoS ONE 9(1):e85110 -- Figure 6 (mineral oil vehicle confound).
claim: Mineral oil (the vehicle used to apply beta-ionone/alpha-ionone in the in vivo experiments) is ITSELF sufficient to increase LNCaP invasiveness and intracellular calcium response via PSGR -- an effect blocked by the PSGR antagonist alpha-ionone, and by PI3K-gamma/Gbeta-gamma inhibitors. In vitro, mineral oil alone vs. mineral oil + beta-ionone were NOT significantly different (negative/null result for beta-ionone's incremental in vitro effect over vehicle alone).
mechanism: A component of mineral oil itself appears to stimulate PSGR (blocked by alpha-ionone, an established PSGR antagonist in this paper's framing, and by PI3K-gamma/Gbeta-gamma inhibitors) -- the same downstream pathway implicated in beta-ionone's effect.
model_system: LNCaP cells, collagen type I gel invasion assay (panel a, n=3) and calcium imaging (panel b, n=7)
verification_notes: NEW record from full-text figure read (PMC3885679, live pull) -- this is exactly the kind of negative/confound finding that is invisible in an abstract and would never have surfaced without reading the actual figure captions and Results text. Explicit quote: 'Results obtained with mineral oil alone and with mineral oil containing beta-ionone are not significantly different' (n=3, two-tailed Student's t-test, control SD=3.67%). Calcium imaging panel (b) used n=7 (control SD=15.96%). IMPORTANT: this means the in vivo mouse experiment (see separate record) does not have a clean vehicle-only negative control -- mineral oil itself activates PSGR, so the 'untreated vs. mineral-oil-vehicle vs. beta-ionone' three-arm design in vivo has a confounded middle arm. This should lower confidence in attributing the FULL in vivo metastasis effect specifically to beta-ionone, though the paper's own data shows beta-ionone still adds an effect beyond the mineral-oil baseline (unique lung/Tyson-gland metastases only in the beta-ionone arm).

citation: Pronin A, Slepak V (2021). "Ectopically expressed olfactory receptors OR51E1 and OR51E2 suppress proliferation and promote cell death in a prostate cancer cell line." J Biol Chem 296:100475 -- receptor/cell-type specificity control.
claim: The cytostatic effect is not unique to OR51E1: LNCaP cells overexpressing OR51E2 (and OR2AT4) also show suppressed proliferation, in contrast to LNCaP cells overexpressing a prototypical Gs-coupled GPCR (beta-2 adrenergic receptor, beta2AR), which did not suppress proliferation. OR51E1 overexpression had NO effect on proliferation of HEK293 cells (a cell type that does not endogenously express these receptors), unlike its effect in LNCaP -- indicating LNCaP cells possess a specific downstream pathway coupling these ORs to proliferation control, not a generic overexpression-toxicity artifact.
mechanism: Receptor-specificity established via FLAG-tagged expression-level comparison across OR51E1, OR51E2, OR2AT4, and beta2AR in LNCaP; cell-type specificity established via HEK293 negative control.
model_system: LNCaP cells (stable inducible OR51E2/OR2AT4/beta2AR overexpression) and HEK293 cells (negative control)
verification_notes: NEW record from full-text read (PMC8024707, live pull), directly confirming the base record's claim that OR51E2 itself (not just OR51E1) is implicated -- verified this explicitly since the paper's figures are titled around OR51E1 specifically (e.g. 'Figure 6: Overexpression of OR51E1 inhibits LNCaP cell proliferation'), which could otherwise be misread as an OR51E1-only finding. The Discussion states explicitly: 'Our data are unexpected because they show that OR51E1 and OR51E2 not only do not promote growth but also are toxic to cells... supported by both the overexpression and knockdown of the endogenous receptors.' No replicate/N count given for the OR51E2-specific comparison panel in the text reviewed; the general LNCaP doubling time reported elsewhere in the paper is ~28 hours under untreated control conditions, for reference.
raw_excerpt_or_link: doi:10.1016/j.jbc.2021.100475, Results section 'Ligand, receptor, and cell-type specificity of OR-mediated cytostasis'

citation: Neuhaus EM, Zhang W, Gelis L, Deng Y, Noldus J, Hatt H (2009). "Activation of an olfactory receptor inhibits proliferation of prostate cancer cells." J Biol Chem 284(24):16218-16225 -- Figure 3 (PSGR-siRNA receptor-specificity knockdown).
claim: PSGR-siRNA knockdown in LNCaP cells reduces the beta-ionone-induced intracellular Ca2+ signal by ~80% and the ADT-induced signal similarly, confirming that both ligand responses are PSGR-dependent rather than off-target chemical effects. Expression of GFP alone or scrambled control siRNA did not alter the beta-ionone response.
mechanism: siRNA-mediated PSGR knockdown (confirmed by reduced immunohistochemical PSGR staining) attenuates ligand-induced Ca2+ signaling, isolating the receptor as the necessary mediator.
model_system: LNCaP cells, PSGR-siRNA/GFP co-transfection vs. non-transfected neighboring cells
verification_notes: NEW record from full-text read (PMC web article, PMC2713531) -- this receptor-specificity knockdown control was not captured in the original abstract-derived record. Real N values: beta-ionone condition n=22 (siRNA) vs n=186 (control) cells, from 4 independent transfections; ADT condition n=26 (siRNA) vs n=201 (control) cells, from 4 independent experiments. sample_size field uses the larger control-arm N (186) as representative; see claim/mechanism for the full n breakdown across both ligand conditions.
raw_excerpt_or_link: doi:10.1074/jbc.M109.012096, Figure 3 and Results text (PMC web article)

citation: Neuhaus EM, Zhang W, Gelis L, Deng Y, Noldus J, Hatt H (2009). "Activation of an olfactory receptor inhibits proliferation of prostate cancer cells." J Biol Chem 284(24):16218-16225 -- Figure 4E-F (p38/SAPK-JNK MAPK inhibitor rescue).
claim: Pharmacological inhibition of p38 and SAPK/JNK MAPKs (but not ERK1/2, which was not activated) abolishes beta-ionone's antiproliferative effect on LNCaP cells, confirming these two kinases are necessary mediators. Neither inhibitor affected proliferation of non-stimulated LNCaP cells at the concentrations used.
mechanism: p38 inhibitor active near its reported IC50 (used at 0.5uM vs. IC50=0.38uM); SAPK/JNK inhibitor required a much higher concentration than its IC50 to be effective (used 1uM vs. IC50=40nM for JNK-1/2, 90nM for JNK3) -- partial effects only at lower concentrations (not shown in main figures). ERK1/2 (p44/42) was tested and showed no phosphorylation change, ruling it out as a mediator in this system.
model_system: LNCaP cells, pharmacological MAPK inhibitor co-treatment with beta-ionone
verification_notes: NEW record from full-text read (PMC web article, PMC2713531). This is the mechanistic rescue experiment that pins down p38/SAPK-JNK (not ERK1/2/p44-42) as necessary for the antiproliferative effect -- important because it directly corrects the original record's mechanism field, which had incorrectly implied p44/42 involvement. No N/replication count given explicitly for this specific inhibitor-rescue experiment in the text reviewed.
raw_excerpt_or_link: doi:10.1074/jbc.M109.012096, Figure 4E-F and Results text (PMC web article)

citation: Xie S, Shan S, Zhang Y, Sun L, Zhao X, Ma H, Liu M, Zhang Y, Zhu Y, Liu Z (2019). "Beta-ionone suppresses prostate cancer cell growth by targeting p38/JNK-mediated AR-Ser650 phosphorylation and blocking androgen receptor nuclear translocation." Cancer Lett 459:36-46.
claim: Beta-ionone (a known OR51E2 agonist) suppresses prostate cancer cell growth both in vitro (LNCaP, PC-3) and in vivo (xenograft) by activating p38 and JNK kinases, which phosphorylate the androgen receptor (AR) at Ser650 — a modification that blocks AR nuclear translocation and transcriptional activation of proliferative genes.
mechanism: Beta-ionone → OR51E2 → p38/JNK activation → AR-Ser650 phosphorylation → blocked AR nuclear translocation → inhibited AR-dependent transcription → growth suppression. Mechanistically distinct from Neuhaus 2009 (which showed p38/SAPK-JNK activation but did not identify AR-Ser650 as the downstream target).
model_system: LNCaP, PC-3 cells (in vitro); xenograft mouse model (in vivo)
verification_notes: Verified from abstract and title (Cancer Lett 459:36-46, 2019; PMID 31185268). Title, claim, and mechanism all match abstract text. Direction (tumor-suppressive) and endpoint (proliferation/growth) are unambiguous from the paper title itself. Mechanism (AR-Ser650 phosphorylation blocking nuclear translocation) is a distinctive downstream target not present in Neuhaus 2009, making this an independent mechanistic claim rather than a restatement. In vivo xenograft arm strengthens the evidence. Not yet verified against full-text figures for exact N values — treat replication_count and sample_size as unknown pending full read.

citation: Xu X, Khater M, Wu G (2022). "The olfactory receptor OR51E2 activates ERK1/2 through the Golgi-localized Gβγ-PI3Kγ-ARF1 pathway in prostate cancer cells." Front Pharmacol 13:1009380.
claim: Beta-ionone dose-dependently activates ERK1/2 in prostate cancer cells (EC50 ~20 μM) via a Golgi-localized Gβγ-PI3Kγ-ARF1 pathway; CRISPR knockout of Gγ9, PI3Kγ, or ARF1, and pharmacological inhibition of Gβγ (gallein) or PI3Kγ (AS-604850), each abolishes beta-ionone-induced ERK1/2 activation. No ERK1/2 response in HEK293 (OR51E2-negative) cells.
mechanism: Beta-ionone → OR51E2 → Gβγ translocation to Golgi → PI3Kγ → ARF1 → ERK1/2 phosphorylation. Spatially coordinated at the Golgi apparatus.
model_system: Prostate cancer cell lines (DU145 and others); HEK293 negative control
verification_notes: PRELIMINARY — abstract/PMC skim only, not Person A full-text verified. Mechanism paper (ERK activation) rather than direct proliferation/invasion endpoint; direction inferred as tumor-promoting from MAPK hyper-activation framing and alignment with Sanz PI3Kγ/Gβγ pathway. Does NOT directly measure proliferation — treat as supporting mechanistic context for the contested activation narrative, not as independent phenotypic evidence.

citation: Abaffy T, Bain JR, Muehlbauer MJ, Spasojevic I, Lodha S, Bruguera E, O'Neal SK, Kim SY, Matsunami H (2018). "A Testosterone Metabolite 19-Hydroxyandrostenedione Induces Neuroendocrine Trans-Differentiation of Prostate Cancer Cells via an Ectopic Olfactory Receptor." Front Oncol 8:162.
claim: Screened 24 OR51E2 agonists and 1 antagonist; endogenous agonist 19-hydroxyandrostenedione (19-OH AD, aromatase product) activation decreases cell viability, induces cell cycle arrest, and upregulates neuronal/neuroendocrine (NE) markers (e.g. neuron-specific enolase). NE marker induction abolished in OR51E2-KO cells.
mechanism: OR51E2 agonist activation → decreased intracellular anabolic signals → cell cycle arrest + neuroendocrine trans-differentiation (NEtD). 19-OH AD is endogenously produced upon receptor activation in LNCaP cells.
model_system: LNCaP prostate cancer cells; OR51E2-KO validation
verification_notes: PRELIMINARY — abstract-level only, not Person A full-text verified. Direction is ambiguous for clinical interpretation: decreased viability/cell cycle arrest reads tumor-suppressive on proliferation, but NE trans-differentiation is associated with aggressive CRPC/NEPC phenotypes (non-mitotic NE-like cells may provide paracrine growth support to surrounding carcinoma). Load as tumor-suppressive with this NE-differentiation caveat — do not fold into high-confidence activation mass until verified.
endpoint: viability, cell cycle arrest, NE marker expression
